Abstract

Background: Water resource is a major limiting factor impacted by climate change that threatens crop production and quality. Understanding the ecophysiological mechanisms involved in the response to water deficit is crucial to select new varieties more drought tolerant. A major bottleneck hampering such advances is the lack of methods for measuring ecophysiological traits on large populations of individuals. We investigated the relevance of spectroscopy and porofluorometry with related high-throughput measurement devices to predict leaf morphological and ecophysiological traits using partial least square regression (PLSR) models. This work relies on a grapevine diversity panel grown in pots under contrasted conditions, outdoors under well watered conditions and in a greenhouse with three different soil water treatments. We took advantage of these experimental designs to specifically assess the robustness of predictive models.

Results: Some complementarity between measuring devices were found, with spectrometers being able to predict leaf mass per area, water content and water quantity (R²>0.58), while the poro-fluorometer could predict net CO2 assimilation (R²>0.72), regardless of the water treatment. The prediction of leaf mass per area appeared to be quite robust between outdoors and greenhouse experiments. The prediction of water use efficiency was highly dependent on the water treatment, with much better predictions under moderate (R²>0.73) than severe water deficit. We then used the high-throughput devices alone to measure the whole grapevine panel, by applying calibration models to predict ecophysiological traits and estimated their broad sense heritability. Leaf mass per area was also directly determined on the entire diversity panel and its

Conclusions: This study showed that spectrometers and poro-fluorometer could be reliable nondestructive tools for the high-throughput phenotyping of ecophysiological traits on thousands of plants, paving the way for studying the genetic determinism of such traits.

Introduction

Over the last decades, climate change has become a global concern. The Mediterranean region is particularly concerned, where prediction expects significant temperature rises and changes in annual rainfall patterns [1,2]. In order to maintain crops in this context, there is a need to explore new combinations of genetic and management choices depending on environmental conditions. Identifying the best performing solutions further requires high throughput characterization of traits related to plant functioning and their responses to the environment.

Gas exchange, leaf structure and specific leaf biochemical components are among the traits most impacted by abiotic constraints such as soil water availability. The regulation of gas exchange at the leaf level by stomatal closure/opening plays a key role in the whole plant response to water stress. Indeed, a soil water deficit leads to a drop in water potential all along the plant that could damage its water transfer capacity and result in organ or plant death [3]. To prevent such damage, plants can quickly close their stomata, reducing transpiration (E) [4] and helping to maintain a high leaf water content (WCf) [5]. However, this water saving strategy has a heavy cost for the plant as it also decreases net photosynthesis (An) by decreasing CO2 uptake [3], and in turn biomass production. A trade-off between reducing E and maintaining An under water stress can be sought by considering the water use efficiency, i.e. the biomass produced per unit of water consumed. At the leaf level, it is measured with the instantaneous water use efficiency (WUEinst), calculated as the ratio between An and E, while the intrinsic water use efficiency (WUEintr),

63 calculated as the ratio between An and the stomatal conductance for water vapor (gsw), provides 64 an alternative estimate, less dependent on air vapor pressure deficit and closer to the genetic 65 determinisms than WUEinst [6].

In grapevine, the species of interest in the present work, a large genotypic variability has already been observed for An, gsw, WUEintr, leaf water potential ( Ѱ ) [7-10]. However, this variability was observed for a limited number of cultivars and/or in a limited number of growing conditions, usually well-watered conditions. High-throughput assessment of these ecophysiological traits is limited by the time required for measurements using leaf gas analyzers or pressure chambers. Studying large populations with a wide range of genetic diversity under various conditions is crucial to identify the best performing cultivars. It can also enable the identification of genetic regions associated with these traits and their responses to contrasted environmental conditions [10,11]. A main challenge is thus to investigate the use of alternative high-throughput methods as proxies to accurately predict traits of interest on large panels of genotypes under various water availability treatments and under different growing conditions [12].

Fluorometer devices, measuring the quantum efficiency of photosystem II, electron transport rate, and photochemical and non-photochemical quenching coefficients, have been proposed as a low-cost and fast method to estimate An [13,14]. Chlorophyll fluorescence measurements were also reported to evaluate the variability of photosynthetic performance among a population of hybrids in barley [15]. Moreover, previous studies have calculated a semi-empirical index that combines fluorescence parameters and leaf temperature, to estimate net photosynthesis in fruit trees [16]. This indicator was then used to evaluate the photosynthesis activity of a core-collection of apples in response to water deficit [17]. Near-infrared spectroscopy (NIRS) is another highthroughput method commonly used to predict plant tissue composition [18-20]. Dedicated chemometrics methods, such as Partial Least Square Regression (PLSR) [21], are used to establish relationships between spectral absorbance or reflectance and reference measurements

NIRS has been reported in plants to predict numerous leaf compositional traits, including nitrogen [22] and carbon content, lignin, cellulose [19], protein, starch, total non-structural carbohydrates [23] and sugars content [24,25]. NIRS has also been implemented to predict various morphological and ecophysiological traits on multiple species and on large populations of the same species. For instance, leaf mass per area (LMA), which is widely used in plant ecology as an indicator of plant functioning, has been predicted by NIRS on diverse species [24,26]. NIRS predictive models were also reported for LMA and An on wheat and triticale germplasms [27] as well as on hybrids of maize [28]. LMA, An, WUEintr, WUEinst , Ѱ and leaf water content were together predicted by NIRS on a diversity panel of maize [22] and Ѱ has also been predicted by NIRS on several cultivars of grapevine [29,30]. The relevance of NIRS to predict water related traits was observed to be associated with light absorption by water at wavelength bands centered around 1400 and 1900 nm [31,32]. However, the presence of water can also reduce leaf reflectance, hindering the detection of other components. To minimize the impact of water on spectra and consequently on the prediction accuracy of other traits, spectra can be collected on dried leaves [33]. Alternatively, the water absorption effect can be reduced using water-removed spectra [34] or by using an external parameter orthogonalisation [35]. Finally, not all spectrometers measure the same wavelength range. Therefore, the type of spectrometer must be selected according to its application [36,37].

106 When studying ecophysiological traits, which vary according to experimental conditions, the 107 robustness of PLSR model predictions must be examined. On grapevine, predictive models with 108 NIRS data have been built on few cultivars subjected to different soil water treatments [29,30,38]. 109 PLSR models were calibrated and validated with datasets containing samples from all water 110 treatments [29,38], without assessing the ability of the model calibrated on a given water 111 treatment to predict the values of plants subjected to another soil water treatment. If PLSR models 112 are robust across different aerial environmental conditions and soil water availabilities,

113 phenotyping time could be reduced in new experiments. Another issue with the PLSR model is 114 the risk of overfitting. To prevent this risk, the calibration model is usually built with a cross115 validation procedure. However, even in a cross-validation setting, the predictive ability of the 116 model could still depend on the calibration and validation sets that could be randomly or non117 randomly chosen [39].

In this study, we evaluated the ability and complementarity of NIRS and poro-fluorometry data to predict leaf traits that are difficult to access at high-throughput. These two innovative approaches were applied on a grapevine diversity panel [40], grown in pots both outdoors under non-limiting water availability and in a greenhouse with varying intensities of soil water deficit. The robustness of PLSR models was studied across experiments and water treatments. Heritabilities of traits either directly measured or estimated from high-throughput data were compared to investigate the ability of high-throughput phenotyping to assess the genotypic variability.

Material and methods

Plant material and experimental design

127 We used a subset of 241 cultivars from a panel of 279, previously designed to represent the 128 worldwide genetic diversity of Vitis vinifera [40]. We added 5 cultivars that are in the process of 129 being registered in the French catalog, resulting in 246 studied cultivars. Twenty own-rooted 130 plants were grown for each cultivar with ferti-irrigation from 2018 at the experimental vineyard of 131 l'Institut Agro Montpellier (France) in individual 3-liter pots containing a 30:70 (v/v) mixture of 132 loamy soil and organic compost [41]. In January 2020, the plants were transferred into larger, 133 individual 9-liter pots filled with the same substrate. 134 A first phenotyping season was conducted in July 2021, on the potted plants grown in the 135 experimental vineyard under non-limiting, drip-irrigated conditions ('Outdoor experiment'). Six plants out of the 20 available per cultivar were next retained to be part of a greenhouse experiment in 2022, where different watering treatments were set up ('Greenhouse experiment'). This second experiment was conducted in the PhenoArch phenotyping platform [42] hosted at the M3P (Montpellier Plant Phenotyping Platforms, https://eng-lepse.montpellier.hub.inrae.fr/platformsm3p/montpellier-plant-phenotyping-platforms-m3p), from May to August 2022. The whole panel (246 cultivars) was subjected to three different water treatments: Well-Watered (WW), moderate Water Deficit (WD1) and severe Water Deficit (WD2) maintained at different soil water content in the pots, with two plants for each cultivar and each water treatment (1476 plants in total). These treatments correspond to soil water potential equal to -0.03 MPa (well watered conditions, WW), -0.11 Mpa (moderate water deficit, WD1) and -0.43 Mpa (severe water deficit, WD2) (protocol for management of water treatment as in Coupel-Ledru et al., 2014 [10]). For each cultivar, the six plants were grouped into two triplets (one triplet including one WW, one WD1 and one WD2 plant), and cultivar triplets were randomized within the phenotyping platform. Leaf phenotyping was conducted over 24 mornings in June and July 2022, once the water content in the pots had stabilized at their target values. Environmental variables were monitored using 10 probes, strategically distributed throughout the greenhouse.

Leaf phenotyping

For both experiments (outdoors and greenhouse), measurements were performed between 9:00 and 13:00 (UTC+2), maximizing environmental and traits variations to support the statistical calibration process. Leaves were chosen as fully expanded and well exposed to sunlight. They were usually located between the tenth and the fifteenth phytomer from the apex. Two kinds of measurements were performed: high-throughput phenotyping on all six plants of the 246 cultivars and low-throughput phenotyping, on a subset of randomly selected plants. The first device used was a hand-held poro-fluorometer (Li600, Portable Photosynthesis System LI-600, Licor, Lincoln, United States). It was used to measure the transpiration rate, E (mmol m -2 s -1 ), the stomatal conductance gsw (mol m -2 s -1 ), chlorophyll fluorescence parameters and micro-climate variables i.e. leaf vapor pressure deficit, atmospheric pressure and ambient light (Supplementary Table 1). Two measurements were taken at two different points on the same leaf and were averaged to obtain a single value per leaf. The second and third devices were micro-portable near infrared spectrometers: MicroNIR (MicroNIR™ OnsiteW, VIAVI, Chandler, AZ, United States) and NeoSpectra (NeoSpectra™ Scanner, Si-Ware, Miami, FL, United States). The MicroNIR covers a spectral range from 908 nm to 1676 nm with 125 wavelengths, resulting in a spectral sampling interval of 6.2 nm. The NeoSpectra covers a spectral range from 1350 nm to 2500 nm with 257 wavelengths and a spectral sampling interval of 16 nm. The wavelength interval between 1350 nm and 1676 nm was common between both devices. For both devices, two spectra were collected per leaf on the lamina while avoiding the principal veins. These two spectra were respectively collected on each side of the leaf (abaxial and adaxial faces) and were then averaged to obtain a single spectrum per leaf. After these measurements, a 3 cm diameter disk was sampled from the leaf. In the greenhouse experiment, this disk was immediately weighed after sampling to obtain its fresh weight (FW, mg) with 0.1 mg accuracy (Sartorius balance, QUINTIX224-1S, Göttingen, Germany). For both experiments, the disk was oven-dried for one week at 60°C before being weighed to get its dry weight (DW, mg). Leaf Mass per Area (LMA, mg cm - ²) was calculated as the ratio between DW and disk area (7.07 cm²). For disks of leaves collected in the greenhouse experiment only, leaf water content (WCf, mg H2O mg -1 fresh weight) was also calculated, as 𝐹𝑊 - 𝐷𝑊 𝐹𝑊 and the water quantity (WQ, mg H2O per leaf disk) as 𝐹𝑊-𝐷𝑊 . The measurement of LMA, WCf, and WQ is traditionally considered a low-throughput method due to the significant time and sample storage requirements. However,

The high186 throughput leaf phenotyping was performed during 24 mornings on the greenhouse experiment. 187 After weighing, dried disks were stored at 20°C. They were later used again to collect NIRS 188 spectra using a laboratory spectrometer ASD (ASD LabSpec® 4, Analytical Spectral Devices, 189 Boulder, USA). The temperature was maintained at 20°C in the lab during measurements. Four 190 spectra were taken using a reflectance probe (4 mm diameter) at different points of the dried disk 191 before being averaged. The ASD covers a spectral range from 350 nm to 2500 nm with 2150 192 wavelengths and a sampling interval of 1 nm. 193 The low-throughput measurements presented in this paper were performed only in the 194 greenhouse experiment on a subset of 120 plants (34 cultivars) subjected to three water 195 treatments. They were performed during 4 mornings (June 14, 2022, July 6, 2022, July 21, 2022 196 and July 26, 2022). The measurements started as described above for the high-throughput 197 devices (i.e. Li600, MicroNir, NeoSpectra), and were followed by a measure with an InfraRed 198 Gas-exchange Analyzer (IRGA) equipped with a light-emitting diode light source (Li6800, Portable 199 Photosynthesis System, Licor, Lincoln, United States). This device allowed measuring net CO2 200 assimilation (An , µmol m -2 s -1 ), transpiration rate (E, mmol m -2 s -1 ) and stomatal conductance (gsw, 201 mol m -2 s -1 ). Intrinsic Water Use Efficiency (WUEintr, µmol CO2 mol -1 H2O) and instantaneous 202 Water Use Efficiency (WUEinst, µmol CO2 mmol -1 H2O) were further calculated as the ratio 203 between An and gsw or An and E, respectively. The conditions in the leaf chamber during the 204 measurement were set to 400 µmol mol -1 CO2, with actinic light adjusted to a value corresponding 205 to the ambient Photosynthetically Photon Flux Density (PPFD) measured simultaneously by the 206 Li600 device for each measured leaf. PPFD values thus ranged from 110 to 1150 µmol m -2 s -1 . 207 The leaf water potential ( Ѱ , MPa) was next measured using a pressure chamber (Scholander 208 pressure bomb, SoilMoisture Equipment Corp., Goleta, United States). Finally, leaf disks were 209 sampled and oven-dried to access LMA and collect spectra with the ASD device.

Preprocessing of spectral and fluorescence data

All data analyses were handled in R v4.3.0 [43]. Spectra from the ASD laboratory spectrometer were initially processed using the rmgap function (R/rchemo package) [44] to remove vertical gaps caused by differences between detectors. Wavelengths between 350 and 400 nm were removed and excluded from spectral analyses due to their noisy signals [45]. The spectra were then smoothed using R/loess, with the neighborhood size controlled by an α value of 0.03. In order to express spectra from all devices in reflectance and wavelength (nm), the MicroNIR absorbance (A) was converted into reflectance (R), as 𝑅 = 1 10 𝐴 , and the NeoSpectra wavenumber (ν, cm -1 ) was converted into wavelength (λ, nm), as 𝜆 = 10 7 𝜐 . As the MicroNIR and NeoSpectra spectrometers have complementary ranges, with an overlap from 1350 nm to 1676 nm, a new, additional spectrum called MicroNIR_NeoSpectra was created for each leaf. Whole MicroNIR spectra from 950 nm to 1676 nm were retained due to their higher resolution than NeoSpectra spectra and combined with the 1676 nm - 2500 nm ranges measured with the NeoSpectra. The resulting gap at the junction of the two spectra was removed using R/rmgap from R/rchemo. All spectra (ASD, MicroNIR, NeoSpectra and MicroNIR_NeoSpectra) were then preprocessed using Standard Normal Variate (SNV) [46]. This was followed by a first-order derivative using the Savitzky-Golay filter (R/sgolayfilt from R/signal package, [47,48]). The polynomial order for the first-order derivative was set to 2, and the window sizes were set respectively to 11, 15, 15 and 41 for MicroNIR, NeoSpectra, MicroNIR_NeoSpectra and ASD, to account for their differences in

Data modeling and cross-validation

234 Calibration models were constructed in order to relate high-throughput data from the 235 spectrometers and the poro-fluorometer Li600 to the traits of interest measured with conventional, 236 low-throughput devices or methods (IRGA, pressure chamber, disks weighing) using Partial Least 237 Square Regression (PLSR), as implemented in R/pls with the 'kernelpls' method [49]. Calibration 238 was performed for the following traits: LMA, WCf, WQ, An, Ѱ , WUEintr and WUEinst, using data from 239 the greenhouse experiment. For the outdoors experiment, only LMA was considered as the other 240 traits were not measured. The number of leaves used to calibrate the model varied from 72 to 241 1437, depending on available data for the measured trait and the cross-validation settings (Table 242 1). Cross-validation was performed using either a 4-fold, 5-fold, or leave-one-out (LOO) method 243 [50]. Four- and 5-fold cross-validations were repeated 10 times (Table 1). 244 PLSR models were built with a maximum number of latent variables set at 20 for spectrometers 245 and 10 for the Li600. The optimal number of latent variables was determined by minimizing the 246 average Root Mean Squared Error (RMSE) over the 10 iterations of the 4- and 5-fold cross247 validations or directly the RMSE for the LOO cross-validation. Model quality was evaluated either 248 within the cross-validation or using validation (testing) sets (see section below for details on the 249 different calibration and validation sets tested) using both the R 2 computed as the squared 250 correlation between observed and predicted values, and the RMSE.

Devices complementarity

The first analyses aimed to compare the predictive performance of PLSR models across the different devices for each trait, using only data from the greenhouse experiment. The calibration set included the same number of leaves for each water treatment, consisting respectively of approximately 480 leaves (dataset A in Table 1) or 36 leaves (dataset B in Table 1) for traits measured at high-throughput (LMA, WCf and WQ) or low-throughput (LMA, An, Ѱ , WUEintr and WUEinst), respectively. A 5-fold cross-validation was conducted based on cultivars rather than individual leaves to avoid the confounding effect of cultivar prediction on the trait prediction. PLSR models were compared based on their cross-validated R²cv, cross-validated normalized root mean square error NRMSEcv= 𝑅𝑀𝑆𝐸𝑐𝑣 𝑥 (with 𝑥 the mean of the trait) and using a Student's t test, as implemented in R/rstatix.

267 Robustness between experiments

268 LMA, was the trait used to assess the robustness of the PLSR models between experiments 269 (oudoors or in the greenhouse). Three calibration sets were used; each with a fixed size of 370 270 leaves (Table 1). The first calibration set (dataset C) included only outdoors leaves, the second 271 one (dataset D) included only greenhouse data, with approximately 123 leaves from each water 272 treatment and the last one (dataset E) included data from both outdoors and greenhouse 273 experiments.

274 The predictive ability of the PLSR models was assessed using validation sets, each including 100 275 leaves, selected to represent experiments and water treatments. The composition of the validation 276 set was determined through random sampling, which accounted for the proportion of plants 277 phenotyped each day in order to be representative of the entire phenotyping series. This sampling 278 was repeated 15 times, resulting in 15 distinct calibration and validation sets. PLSR models were

279 compared based on their R² and RMSE and a Tukey HSD post-hoc test, as implemented in 280 R/stats.

281 Robustness between water treatments

282 Data from the greenhouse experiment were used to assess the robustness of the PLSR models 283 between the different water treatments. For LMA, WCf and WQ, which were phenotyped at high284 throughput, 4 calibration sets were tested, each including a fixed number of 370 leaves, randomly 285 selected independently of cultivar. A separate calibration set was created for each water treatment 286 (datasets F to H in Table 1) and an additional calibration set (dataset I) that included the three 287 water treatments in equal proportions of 123 leaves was also considered. 288 The predictive ability of the PLSR models was assessed using validation sets, each including 100 289 leaves per water treatment and accounting for the proportion of plants phenotyped each day in 290 order to be representative of the entire phenotyping series. This sampling was repeated 15 times, 291 resulting in 15 distinct calibration and validation sets. 292 For An, Ѱ , WUEintr, WUEinst, which were phenotyped at low-throughput, the low number of leaves 293 did not allow us to build calibration sets per water treatment. Thus, calibration sets were created 294 including 24 leaves from each water treatment (dataset J in Table 1). 295 The predictive ability of the PLSR models was assessed using validation sets, each including 10 296 leaves per water treatment and accounting for the proportion of plants phenotyped each day in 297 order to be representative of the entire phenotyping series. This sampling was repeated 100 298 times, resulting in 100 distinct calibration and validation sets, in order to provide reliable estimates 299 and low risk of unrepresentative sampling. Significant differences between predictive abilities 300 were assessed with a Tukey HSD post-hoc test, as implemented in R/stats.

Calculation of WUE using predicted A

Intrinsic Water Use Efficiency (WUEintr_cal) and instantaneous Water Use Efficiency (WUEinst_cal) were calculated as the ratios between predicted An and the measured gsw or E from the Li600 device, respectively. It was done for each validation set of the dataset J (Table 1). The prediction accuracy was obtained by comparing these calculated WUEintr_cal and WUEinst_cal with WUEintr and WUEinst obtained with the Li6800 measurements. Significance between predictive abilities were assessed with a Tukey HSD post-hoc test, as implemented in R/stats.

Variability of leaf ecophysiological traits

The lowest coefficient of variation was observed for WCf (<5%), regardless of the water treatment. LMA and WQ showed intermediate coefficients of variation (between 15% and 22%), with generally slight differences between water treatments and experiments (greenhouse vs. outdoors) (Supplementary Table 2). LMA and WQ were positively correlated, with their correlation coefficient increasing from 0.63 to 0.73 with the intensity of water deficit in the greenhouse experiment (Supplementary Figure 1a, Supplementary Figure 1b and Supplementary Figure 1c). LMA and WCf were negatively correlated, regardless of the water treatment, with correlation coefficient decreasing from -0.57 to -0.48 when water deficit increased. The correlation between WCf and WQ was lower but remained significant and positively different from zero, with values close to 0.25 (Supplementary Figure 1a, Supplementary Figure 1b and Supplementary Figure 1c).

344 An, Ѱ , WUEintr and WUEinst displayed more variability than LMA, WCf and WQ (Supplementary 345 Figure 2), as underlined by higher coefficients of variation (Supplementary Table 2). An was the 346 trait whose coefficient of variation was the most affected by the water treatment, increasing as 347 the water deficit increased. Conversely, the coefficient of variation of WUEintr decreased with

348 increasing water deficit. A n was not significantly correlated with WUEintr and WUEinst, whatever the 349 water treatment (Supplementary Figure 1d, Supplementary Figure 1e and Supplementary Figure 350 1f). WUEintr was strongly correlated with WUEinst with values around 0.8 in all conditions

- 351 (Supplementary Figure 1d, Supplementary Figure 1e and Supplementary Figure 1f).

352 Devices complementarity for the prediction of leaf traits

353 Overall, spectra of the three spectrometers collected in the greenhouse experiment (Datasets A 354 and B in Table 1) were highly effective in predicting traits such as LMA, WCf and WQ. The 355 coefficient of determination computed within the cross-validation procedure consistently 356 exceeded 0.67 (Fig 1a, Supplementary Figure 3a, Supplementary Figure 3b and Supplementary 357 Figure 3c) with almost no variability across the cross-validation repetitions. Nevertheless, 358 significant differences in R² cv were found between devices (Supplementary Table 3). The ASD 359 spectrometer on dry leaves yielded the most accurate models to predict LMA compared to micro360 portable spectrometers used in situ (Fig. 1, Supplementary Figure 4a). In contrast, the best 361 models for predicting WCf and WQ were established with spectra from micro-portable 362 spectrometers collected in situ (MicroNIR, NeoSpectra and MicroNIR_NeoSpectra in Fig. 1a). 363 Although model performances were almost similar for these three modalities, slightly better 364 predictions were achieved by combining the wavelengths from MicroNIR and NeoSpectra (Fig. 365 1). The PLSR models built with the variables from the Li600 device were unable to predict LMA, 366 WCf and WQ (R²cv ≤ 0.1, Fig. 1a). 367 Regarding the prediction of An, Ѱ , WUEintrand WUEinst with spectrometers, the R² values remained 368 low and not higher than 0.27 whatever the device considered (Fig. 1b). In contrast, the models 369 built with variables from the Li600 device for predicting An displayed an R²cv of 0.82 and an 370 NRMSEcvof 0.25 (Fig. 1b, Supplementary Figure 4b, Supplementary Figure 3d). The Li600 device 371 also seemed to capture relevant information for predicting WUEintr and WUEinst, with R²cv values

372 of 0.48 and 0.3, respectively (Fig. 1b, Supplementary Figure 3f and Supplementary Figure 3g). 373 Yet, it is noteworthy that the prediction accuracies of WUEinst using either the Li600 or the 374 MicroNIR_NeoSpectra were not significantly different (Fig. 1b and Supplementary Table 3). Ѱ 375 was poorly predicted, regardless of the device used, with an R²cv always below 0.24 (Fig. 1b and 376 Supplementary Figure 3e). The large variability in R²cv or NRMSEcv between the different cross377 validation sets was attributed to the small number of leaves included in each set (Fig. 1, dataset 378 B in Table 1).

379 To further investigate the effect of calibration set size on model accuracy, we compared PLSR 380 models built with low- (109 leaves) and high-throughput (1437 leaves) measurements of LMA. As 381 expected, models built within the low-throughput calibration set displayed significantly lower R² cv 382 than models built within the high-throughput calibration set (by 0.08 to 0.15) (Supplementary 383 Figure 5a). This might partly be due to a lower range of variation in the low-throughput calibration 384 set compared to the high-throughput calibration set (Supplementary Figure 1h). This point is 385 confirmed by the RMSEcv values which displayed low differences between lowand high386 throughput calibration sets with values always lower than 0.42 mg cm - ² (Supplementary Figure 387 5b).

Prediction robustness across experiments

The prediction accuracy for LMA (R²) was significantly lower with the outdoors than the greenhouse validation set (Fig. 2). The median R² values of the 15 iterations ranged from 0.30 to 0.86 when models were evaluated with the outdoors validation set and from 0.78 to 0.90 when they were evaluated with the greenhouse validation set when all the devices were considered. The ASD device used on dried disks yielded the most robust models for predicting LMA, with a median R² values always exceeding 0.78, and RMSE values lower than 0.62 mg cm - ², regardless of the calibration and validation sets (Fig. 2). Conversely the models built with micro-portable

403 spectrometers on fresh leaves, especially the MicroNIR, appeared to be impacted by the 404 environmental conditions in which they were calibrated and validated (Fig. 2). This was 405 particularly the case when models were calibrated with greenhouse data and validated on data 406 collected outdoors. For MicroNIR, NeoSpectra and MicroNIR_NeoSpectra devices, the 407 performance of models to predict LMA in outdoors conditions was similarly good whether 408 calibrated with outdoor or greenhouse_outdoor calibration sets with R² always exceeding 0.61 409 and RMSE always below 0.51 mg cm -2 (Fig. 2). Models calibrated with outdoors data remained 410 accurate to predict greenhouse data since R² was greater than 0.78 and RMSE lower than 0.8 411 mg cm - ². However, the prediction of greenhouse data, using an outdoors calibration set 412 significantly reduced R² and RMSE values compared to greenhouse or greenhouse_outdoor 413 calibration sets (p-value ≤ 0.001). Discussion

In this study, we explored the performance of models built with high-throughput devices to predict ecophysiological and morphological leaf traits across a large genetic diversity panel of Vitis vinifera varieties. Using a combination of large datasets collected on thousands of grapevine plants across different experiments and conditions, we were able to evaluate the robustness of predictive models with respect to the experimental conditions. Based on an analytical framework with dedicated and repeated calibration and validation data sets, we found that the prediction accuracy within the validation set containing leaves of plants grown under severe water deficit was generally lower than that for other water treatments. We also found that all devices (NIR spectrometers or poro-fluorometer) were efficient in predicting at least one trait. Finally, we found that the leaf traits predicted with NIRS and poro-fluorometry data captured a substantial part of the genetic variation which paves the way towards their use in genetic studies.

High-throughput devices are complementary to predict a range of morphological and physiological traits

For too small datasets or too complex traits, repeated double cross-validation is more efficient to obtain an accurate estimation of the prediction error compared to a simple crossvalidation [39]. In our analyses, we performed 100 resampling iterations for each cross-validated calibration and validation sets for predicting A n , Ѱ , WUEintr and WUEinst. Previous studies most often only made one iteration for cross-validating PLSR models [25]. In this study, we observed that model R² was highly dependent on the iteration. Consequently, depending on the sampling for unique datasets of calibration and validation, the models could have been biased. Spectrometers resulted in better performances than Li600 for the prediction of LMA, WCf and WQ while the Li600 was more efficient for the prediction of An, WUEintr and WUEinst. The laboratory spectrometer (ASD) was found as the most accurate to predict LMA. Absorption bands between 1670-1830 nm and 2000-2200 nm, covered by this device, were previously reported as relevant

for predicting leaf dry matter related traits like protein, lignin and cellulose contents [53-56]. The difference of predictive performance between the laboratory spectrometer and the portable spectrometers could also be due to the presence/absence of water in the corresponding leaf samples as spectra from the ASD device were collected on dried leaves while spectra from MicroNIR and NeoSpectra devices were collected in situ . It is known that the presence of water in samples can dominate the signal, potentially reducing the detection of smaller peaks related to other components such as nitrogen [33], protein, lignin and cellulose, which could be essential for predicting LMA. LMA and WCf were negatively correlated, likely because leaves with a low LMA tend to have a high water content [57], but we observed that the devices yielding the best PLSR models for predicting these traits differed. Water-related traits (WCf and WQ) were better predicted in situ using portable spectrometers while LMA was better predicted ex situ using a lab spectrometer on dried leaves. Moreover, water-related traits were better predicted using the combined wavelengths of MicroNIR and NeoSpectra than with either device alone. This is likely because their combined spectral range includes the two main water absorption bands around 1400-1440 nm and 1900-1950 nm [29].

The poro-fluorometer device was found to provide good prediction of An. To our knowledge, this is the first report of A n prediction using rapid and simultaneous porometry and fluorescence measurements. This aligns with previous studies showing that An correlates with the fluorescence decline ratio under steady-state conditions with saturated light [58]. Additionally, An was well predicted when combining the electron transport rate, also derived from leaf fluorescence measurements, with the leaf-to-air temperature difference, used as a proxy for stomatal closure [14,16,17]. In our study, spectrometers could not accurately predict An , conversely to other studies on maize [28]. At least 3 spectra from the same side of a leaf were used in the maize experiment, while in our study, two spectra (one on each side) were taken from the same spatial area of a given leaf. De Bei et al. [29] reported that decreasing the number of spectra collected on a given leaf from 6 to 1 decreases the prediction accuracy. However,

We found that the Li600 device was able to predict WUEintr and WUEinst for plants under a moderate water deficit. In contrast, spectral data generally failed to provide reliable predictions, except for WUEinst, which could be predicted using MicroNIR and the MicroNIR-NeoSpectra combination under moderate water deficit. With a larger sample size, Cotrozzi et al. [28] reported fairly good predictive performance of models built with NIRS data by performing 500 randomized permutations for WUEintr and WUEinst on an external validation set of maize.

The leaf water potential is a physiological trait commonly used as an indicator of soil water deficit intensity [3,29,59]. In our study, the leaf water potential was poorly predicted, regardless of the device used. Experiments on maize have shown that the leaf water potential could be predicted by NIRS with a R²cv of 0.63 on both greenhouse and field samples [28]. Field experiments on grapevines have shown the interest of NIRS for the prediction of leaf water potential on Syrah and Cabernet Sauvignon [29]. However, in these studies, stem water potential was better predicted than leaf water potential perhaps because spectra, taken at a single spot on the leaf, are not representative of the spatial variability in stomatal conductance [29,60]. Moreover, in the case of a slow soil drying, the osmotic adjustment leads to a maintenance in water content even when leaf water potential decreases [3] which could explain the good predictive ability of water content compared to leaf water potential.

Recently, the PLSR method was extended to the combination of multiple methods and sources of high-throughput data using deep learning [61] or multi-block techniques such as SOPLS or ROSA [62,63]. The prediction of amylose, starch and cellulose contents, using a SO-PLS model with NIR and mid-infrared spectra, had a better R² and a smaller error compared to the PLSR models based on a NIR and mid-infrared spectra separately [64]. The prediction of E using NIRS data was shown to be improved by incorporating climatic data such as radiation, temperature, relative humidity and wind speed [38]. It has also recently been shown that the

640 prediction of leaf water potential could be improved by combining leaf spectral reflectance and 641 leaf orientation parameters [65]. However, in a high-throughput phenotyping approach, some 642 complementary information such as leaf orientation, might be difficult to phenotype at a large 643 scale.

LMA has been widely used across species and locations to investigate the robustness of predictive models when plants were subjected to different environmental conditions [26,66]. In these studies, a considerable decrease in model accuracy was found when a trained PLSR model was applied to a validation set containing other sites or experiments. Here, we consistently highlighted a degradation of outdoors prediction using a greenhouse calibration set, similar to recent reports on maize [28]. By contrast, a previous interspecific study using more than 700 species, reported that the prediction accuracy for LMA could reach a satisfying stable level with a certain number of sites used in the calibration set [66].

Furthermore, here we aimed at deciphering the effect of the watering conditions on the PLSR models prediction accuracy. The prediction of ecophysiological traits might indeed be sensible to soil water availability. While most published studies pooled together data collected in different growing conditions in their calibration and validation datasets [29,38], here we tested both the effects of calibrating PLSR models per water treatment, and more importantly of validating them under different water treatments. Our results highlighted that the composition of the validation dataset is more influential than the calibration one. Validating PLSR models per water treatment prevents an artificial inflation of R 2 value caused by the inclusion of multiple water treatments which in turn increases the trait variance. This likely led to more accurate PLSR model evaluations which highlighted changes in prediction accuracy for LMA, WCf, WUEintr and WUEinst

664 depending on the water treatments. Such variation in R 2 is primarily due to the reduction in trait 665 variance as underlined by the smaller differences observed for RMSE across water treatments. 666 Yet, differences in RMSE remained and they could be attributed to a differential implication of 667 elementary processes across water treatments, some of them being more or less captured by 668 high-throughput measurements.

669 PLSR models allow capturing the genetic variability of leaf traits

670

671

672

673

674

High correlations between predicted and measured values do not necessarily imply an accurate prediction of genetic variability for the traits, especially when the calibration set includes multiple experimental conditions. In such cases, the model might have been trained to predict differences between experimental treatments rather than between plant-related variation within a given treatment.

675

676

677

678

679

680

681

682

683

684

685

686

687

Our results demonstrated that the broad sense heritability of predicted LMA was comparable to that of measured LMA, underlying, at least for this trait, that predicted values were as efficient as measured values for capturing the genetic variability within each water treatment. Similar findings were reported for chlorophyll content, nitrogen content and specific leaf area, predicted using hyperspectral reflectance data in maize grown under low and high nitrogen conditions [67]. The heritability of predicted An, WUEintr and WUEinst was water treatment dependent with a higher heritability for A n and WUEintr under moderate water deficit compared to WUEinst and other water treatments. One reason to explain these results could be that our study was based on a single leaf per plant even though the choice of the leaf was homogeneous between plants and chosen as well-exposed and fully expanded. Even so, we could not take into account the intra-plant variability between well-exposed or shadowed leaves, their age or even the plant architecture. Increasing the number of measurements is likely to increase trait heritability [68], but this will also extend the phenotyping time, which may not be appropriate in a high-

688 throughput phenotyping strategy. To overcome this problem, other methodologies based on 689 contactless devices could be tested, such as hyper-spectral imaging [69]. Thermal imaging could 690 also be used to assess leaves and canopy temperature as proxies of water stress responses [70]. 691 Architectural and functional traits have successfully been characterized using LiDAR, 692 multispectral and thermal imaging in a large core-collection of apple trees [17], highlighting a 693 strong genotypic effect on all traits, with high broad-sense heritability. A significant effect of the 694 water treatment was also detected on most of the traits. However, when traits exhibit high spatial 695 variability at fine scale, the accuracy of phenotyping using imaging sensors is related to the image 696 resolution. Airborne imaging may also be affected by background noise and atmospheric 697 scattering, which can alter the light absorption features of pigments [71]. Nevertheless, these 698 high-throughput methods are highly effective for phenotyping large numbers of plants and multiple 699 environments in a short time, thus reducing the error associated with the plant circadian cycle. 700 These phenotyping methods open prospects for further analyses of the genetic determinism of 701 ecophysiological traits as recently shown in apple trees [72].

702 Overall our results open promising avenues for further deploying genome-wide 703 association studies on traits predicted from high-throughput measurements.

704

Conclusion

705 Our findings demonstrate the strengths of high-throughput devices for predicting ecophysiological 706 traits not directly accessible at the same throughput in a diversity panel of grapevine under varying 707 experimental conditions. We have highlighted the importance of validating PLSR models for each 708 experimental condition and not being restricted to a single calibration/validation set. This 709 approach can be applied to other abiotic stresses, other populations or even other species. This 710 would require sharing high-throughput dataset collected on multiple species, growth conditions 711 and environments to increase predictive models robustness so that they can be applied more

712 easily to a wide range of study cases. High-throughput phenotyping strategies can be integrated 713 with imaging-based phenotyping or 3D modeling to improve the assessment of inter-plant 714 variability and further enhance our understanding of plant responses to environmental stresses 715 through genetic analyses.

metadata.preprint_title: Robustness of high-throughput prediction of leaf ecophysiological traits using near infra-red spectroscopy and poro-fluorometry

pub_metadata.preprint_title: Robustness of high-throughput prediction of leaf ecophysiological traits using near infra-red spectroscopy and poro-fluorometry
pub_metadata.preprint_authors: Coindre, E.; Boulord, R.; Chir, L.; Freitas, V.; Ryckewaert, M.; Laisne, T.; Bouckenooghe, V.; Lis, M.; Cabrera-Bosquet, L.; Doligez, A.; Simonneau, T.; Pallas, B.; Coupel-Ledru, A.; Segura, V.
pub_metadata.preprint_abstract: Water scarcity is a major threat to crop production and quality. Improving drought tolerance through variety selection requires a deeper understanding of plant ecophysiological responses, but large-scale phenotyping remains a bottleneck. This study assessed the potential of high-throughput tools (spectroscopy and poro-fluorometry) to predict leaf morphological and ecophysiological traits in a grapevine diversity panel grown in pots under well-watered outdoor conditions and under three contrasting soil water treatments in a greenhouse. We found a certain complementarity between measuring devices. Spectrometers could accurately predict leaf mass per area, water content, and water quantity (R{superscript 2} > 0.58), while the poro-fluorometer was efficient for predicting net CO2 assimilation (R{superscript 2} > 0.72), regardless of the water treatment. The prediction of leaf mass per area using spectrometers appeared to be quite robust across both outdoor and greenhouse experiments, while the prediction of water use efficiency was dependent on the water treatment, with much better predictions under moderate (R{superscript 2} > 0.73) than severe water deficit. Calibrated models were then applied to the full diversity panel using only high-throughput measurements to estimate trait values and their broad-sense heritability. Leaf mass per area, also measured directly, showed similar heritability whether based on observed or predicted data. Heritability estimates for predicted traits reached up to 0.5. Overall, our findings support the use of spectroscopy and poro-fluorometry as reliable, non-destructive tools for high-throughput phenotyping, enabling genetic studies on drought-related traits in grapevine.
